title: Screenshot framing question
wikitext:
[]: Screenshot/framing question!             Quote Post by Xard » Fri Aug 21, 2009 5:27 pm I've been "bugged" by two specific shots in the show for *ages* for very different reasons. I'm putting these up here because based on Commentary scripts and storyboards had, uhh, comments on contents of "frame". So I guess some people here may know definite "answer" to these examples. And even if not, well, maybe you can help me: at least we can exhange ideas... Everyone who has watched NGE knows just how wacky or unusual Anno's camera angles and placements can get. As simple example, who the hell (except him because I've seen him use this same angle elsewhere) frames metro door like this? [URL=http://img232.imageshack.us/my.php?image=ngeep11elevatordoorscam.png] [/URL] If one starts to look for these things they just pop up everywhere. Fisheye lenses, floor cameras etc. PICTURE 1 While I'm good at "classifying" these things show has two shots that have nearly driven me mad. First comes from Ep 12 bathroom scene between Ritsuko & Misato where they have heated conversation about Misato's crazy plan to stop Sahaquiel. [URL=http://img39.imageshack.us/my.php?image=ngeannoep12cameraanglef.png] [/URL] Problem is quite simple but annoyingly vague to answer definitively. I see two options: A) Anno "halved" the frame with strange aspect ratio (they pop up in mindfucks and in Komm Süsser Tod sequence. Anno would go further to explore potentialities of strange frame shapes [see this for example] in his following works, especially with Kare Kano and Love&Pop - Shiki Jitsu has its share of these too). There are however couple of problems. To begin with always when Anno doodles around with aspect ratios the "empty" stuff is black, not white. Secondly these strange ratios are never seen in "normal" sequences of show. They're only in mindfucks (ep 16 for example) so it makes absolutely no sense. Thirdly to me that bright "whiteness" seems to somewhat glow and/or invade the "drawn" space though it is really hard to tell Because of this I think B) that it is panel lamp/ceiling light that fills other half of the screen (joys of animation: for live action getting angle like this would be MAJOR pain in the ass if not downright impossible). It makes sense, analyzing the composition, especiall the "glow" of "bright stuff" but as I said I might just be imagining it. So I'm 90% sure right answer would be the latter one but... it is a bit ambiguous still. What do you think? And does official material have anything of relevance? *Do* those bathrooms have such lights? As far as I know we've never seen ceiling in that place. PICTURE 2 okay, that was first one. Second one comes from Ep 23 and this is the real bugger [URL=http://img80.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap197617.jpg] [/URL] Is this simply question of lightning choice that creates "illusion" in my head or is Anno throwing in some delicious "fisheye lenses" that make structure of picture "curved" with center of pic (and the focus point for "light") being like peak of the hill and closer we get to borders of frame more we have descended the slopes? Anno has certainly favoured this oddball camera effect far more than anyone else I've seen. Ultimate example would be this: [URL=http://img232.imageshack.us/my.php?image=ngeannoweirdcameraangle.jpg] [/URL] here's examples from Shiki Jitsu: [URL=http://img216.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap562117.png] [/URL] [URL=http://img215.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap562421.png] [/URL] (effect is much more subtle so you must see those in full resolution to see the effect I guess) I've zoomed in and zoomed out and done all sorts of things and I just can't say whether it is just lighting and that frame is completely flat or whether it is curved ***************************''' So, those are my "wtf's". I have couple of other small questions related to two other pics. BONUS CONTENT [URL=http://img39.imageshack.us/my.php?image=endofevangelionoddcamer.png] [/URL] to left there's pipes but what the heck is that thing on the right half of the frame? Also, where's the camera supposedly placed in that? Is it "wall's" pov? I don't see any other way to make such composition in any case... [URL=http://img87.imageshack.us/my.php?image=endofevangelioncameraan.jpg] [/URL] This is one of most quietly original shots in the whole show. It took me long time to realize that crap, I've never seen shot like this before. Closest other examples would come from Kubrick (for example soldiers looking at camera acting as dead body's pov in FMJ)... Just where the heck is camera positioned in there? It looks like Rei is, uh, "diving" through the frame vertically (for extra lulz and weirdness). Turning pic around has certainly not maked it any less weird. Here's reference shots: [URL=http://img81.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap262678.jpg] [/URL] [URL=http://img216.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap260055.jpg] [/URL] [URL=http://img269.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap262824.jpg] [/URL] [URL=http://img44.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap262843.jpg] [/URL] [URL=http://img38.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap262860.jpg] [/URL] [URL=http://img215.imageshack.us/my.php?image=vlcsnap262882.jpg] [/URL] where would "camera" be located based on sketchy picture of milieu seen here? I thought it was where Shinji's head is but I'm not completely sure... I guess it's in the lap area in general but... gaaahh *mind explodes* Curse you Anno and your wacky framings!

[]:             Quote Post by Great Genius Shinji-Sama » Fri Aug 21, 2009 5:39 pm Lucky ass camera if it is in the lap. Id love to be in Rei's lap!!!

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Fri Aug 21, 2009 5:52 pm There is no camera. DUN DUN DUNNNNNNN~ Seriously, though, Anno-san's taking advantage of the medium. It isn't film, it is animation. There is no camera. ^_~

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Fri Aug 21, 2009 5:54 pm oOoOoOo wrote: There is no camera. DUN DUN DUNNNNNNN~ Seriously, though, Anno-san's taking advantage of the medium. It isn't film, it is animation. There is no camera. ^_~

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Fri Aug 21, 2009 6:29 pm Not very helpful, but input: Picture 1 - yes, cieling light, always thought so at least. Picture 2 - I don't see the forced perspective of a fisheye; the curved couch is a curved couch. The POV is rotated from the 90 degree/parallel of the walls of the room. Maybe there is some forced flattening of the image if it were compared to a camera shot. Bonus content - could be anything attached to the wall with cables hanging down. I dunno. Looking up at Re - Shinji's POV. Looking down at Shinji - Rei's POV. Looking at floating cross - Shinji's POV. Overhead on Shinji/Rei - just overhead shot, doesn't strike me as too unusual.

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Fri Aug 21, 2009 6:33 pm No camera, no film? Whaaaa~ o_O There is no camera. The only camera is the actual camera, which takes pictures of 2D images on a board. It is animation. It doesn't matter where the POV is. The POV could be anything, because nothing is actually there. I'm a little confused as to what we're trying to figure out. ^^;

[]:             Quote Post by schismatics » Fri Aug 21, 2009 6:34 pm To be honest I never really concerned myself with this sort of thing. I just took it in and was like "Aw sweet that looks cool."

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Fri Aug 21, 2009 7:24 pm oOoOoOo wrote: I'm a little confused as to what we're trying to figure out. ^^;

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Fri Aug 21, 2009 8:21 pm Ah, I see, I see. ^^; I think we've got it all pretty much figured out! The close-ups are obviously to create intimacy, and the one with Shinji-kun hiding under the stairs, that really takes you away from any sort of intimacy. Of course, he's so shut off from the world in that scene, I'd reckon Anno-san wanted you to feel similarly removed, sort of indifferent to the soldiers. If the soldiers had been shot from low close shots, or we saw Shinji really close, maybe with sweat beads or something, it probably wouldn't be as effective in conveying the "meh" of our poor little pilot. This thread does make me realize that I'm a huge fan of the framing and layouts of shots in Evangelion. Thanks Xard... and your avatar is ridiculous, by the way. ^_~

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Fri Aug 21, 2009 9:06 pm ...you thought this way further than I did Actually, all I wanted to know were the technical aspects. I only created this thread because Picture 2 has bugged me for over a year by now and decided I might as well put in other random musings But I have no qualms over recent developments so feel free to do whatever you want Picture 1 - yes, cieling light, always thought so at least.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Fri Aug 21, 2009 10:22 pm Xard wrote: thewayneiac wrote: Picture 2: This shot is centered on the floor next to the bed to emphasize that neither of them is sleeping on the floor. i.e., they are comfortable enough with each other to sleep in the same bed. This is in deliberate contrast to the next episode where Shinji does sleep on the floor. He says he just doesn't want to inconvience Kaworu, but it's almost certainly true that he wouldn't feel comfortable sleeping in the same bed with some guy who says he loves him.

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Fri Aug 21, 2009 11:10 pm This is why it's a good anime; because it makes use of the format's specialties! There's nothing to be bugged about if you get out of the "camera" mindset.

[]: Re: Screenshot/framing question!             Quote Post by Bomby von Bombsville » Fri Aug 21, 2009 11:49 pm For the sake of continuity from the original post and the fact that I know much more about live action filmmaking than animation, I'm going to speak of these as if they're live-action sequences. As far as picture one goes, it reminds me of crane shots (not unlike a spectacular one I saw in Inglourious Bastards ) that were used by Brian De Palma in his heyday. My guess is that the right half of the screen was a budget issue, and Anno decided to have the camera behind a "light source" to save time and/or money. I'm really less interested in interpretation than I am in analysis, but if I were to guess a meaning in the way its framed, I would have to say that the two-shot is probably just a preference, as opposed to cross cutting. Placing the POV behind the light source creates a sense of disconnect from the action, making the conversation between Misato and Ritsuko seem more private. For picture two , I think its an illusion from the light source. The couch in the upper right of the frame does give it the extreme wide-angle look, but examining the other corners, I do not see the same look. Especially since the objects toward the upper left and bottom left of the frame seem to contradict the wide-angle look. My interpretation of this framing is that the overall emptiness of the room is supposed to represent Asuka's inner "emptiness" stemming from her depression. The shot simply wouldn't have the same emotional effect if the room were cluttered with objects. As for the first "bonus picture," the question of whether or not there's supposed to be a wall where the camera is placed is of little relevance (to me, anyway). The object on the right hand side of the screen appears to be a security cam. The likely reasoning for these objects is simple: Japanese cinematographers love aperture framing. Once again, my interpretation builds upon the long standing tradition of a high-angle shot usually symbolizing "defeat." Adding the aperture to the frame adds a sense of disconnect from the event, which can cause the audience to feel the same sense of "helplessness" that is diegetically felt by Shinji. Also, another simple explanation is that Shinji is hiding. The apertures are more objects for Shinji to hide behind. As for the sequence at the end, the "portrait" shot of Rei is somewhat baffling. Were it a point of view shot from Shinji's eyes, the hypothetical camera would be at a lower angle and her head would be tilted onward, and the screen would have to be reversed from left to right. This could still be the intent, with Rei's body still appearing from the left for the sake of a strange sense of continuity (which is actually both discontinuous and continuous at the same time - discontinuous within the diegetic space and continuous in that Rei stays on the left of the screen.

[]: Re: Screenshot/framing question!             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Aug 22, 2009 12:22 am Answers! Xard wrote: who the hell (except him because I've seen him use this same angle elsewhere) frames metro door like this? [URL=http://img232.imageshack.us/my.php?image=ngeep11elevatordoorscam.png] [/URL]

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Aug 22, 2009 6:05 am ARRRGGGHHH! Next time you post a thread like this Xard, MAKE SURE I'M AWAKE! After all the excellent analysis and interpretation that's been done I'm left with nothing to really do. :( I guess I can say this about the metro doors: It's interesting that tradition says to film imposing objects from a low angle, but it's almost as if those doors seem more menacing from a high angle. I wonder if Anno realized this? @Bonus Content: The object at the right in the first pic is most certainly a security camera; remember that's how Misato found out Shinji was there and, IIRC, she saw him from roughly the same angle. All of the interps on the Rei/Instrumentality shots were fascinating. I'd never thought much about this before but the whole thing has a very distinct quality where it is detached but perfectly continuous at the same time which gives it the quality of both fantasy and reality, of something really happening, but feeling as if it's more dreamlike.

[]:             Quote Post by Bomby von Bombsville » Sat Aug 22, 2009 10:23 am ^ The "defeat" in high angle and "menace" in low angle can be reversed easily. I feel the need to point out this shot from Citizen Kane : In this shot, a low angle is used to signify the defeat of Kane in the election. (BTW, this is not a spoiler for anyone who hasn't seen Citizen Kane , as it's revealed in the opening newsreel.) Notice how the edges of the ceiling seem to be pointing down at him?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Aug 22, 2009 11:03 am Bomby von Bombsville wrote: ^ The "defeat" in high angle and "menace" in low angle can be reversed easily. I feel the need to point out this shot from Citizen Kane : In this shot, a low angle is used to signify the defeat of Kane in the election. (BTW, this is not a spoiler for anyone who hasn't seen Citizen Kane , as it's revealed in the opening newsreel.) Notice how the edges of the ceiling seem to be pointing down at him?

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Aug 22, 2009 11:56 am Bomby von Bombsville wrote: ^ The "defeat" in high angle and "menace" in low angle can be reversed easily. I feel the need to point out this shot from Citizen Kane :

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sat Aug 22, 2009 11:58 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: After all the excellent analysis and interpretation that's been done I'm left with nothing to really do.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sat Aug 22, 2009 3:34 pm Gahh, talk about thread spiralling completely out of control... why this never happens when I think I make interesting thread (Sephiroth System one as recent example) but when I just ment to inquire few simple things this happens... Writing reply. Includes: - small rant on overbearing "intentionality" and "set psychological responses to visuals" that is taken in my opinion way too far in film study - also why inherent rules bending around this is good thing - comments on comments - randomness?

Article: Screenshot framing question
. Especially since the objects toward the upper left and bottom left of the frame seem to contradict the wide-angle look. My interpretation of this framing is that the overall emptiness of the room is supposed to represent Asuka's inner "emptiness" stemming from her depression. The shot simply wouldn't have the same emotional effect if the room were cluttered with objects. As for the first "bonus picture," the question of whether or not there's supposed to be a wall where the camera is placed is of little relevance (to me, anyway). The object on the right hand side of the screen appears to be a security cam. The likely reasoning for these objects is simple: Japanese cinematographers love aperture framing. Adding the aperture to the frame adds a sense of disconnect from the event, which can cause the audience to feel the same sense of "helplessness" that is diegetically felt by Shinji. Also, another simple explanation is that Shinji is hiding. The apertures are more objects for Shinji to hide behind. As for the sequence at the end, the "portrait" shot of Rei is somewhat baffling. Were it a point of view shot from Shinji's eyes, the hypothetical camera would be at a lower angle and her head would be tilted onward, and the screen would have to be reversed from left to right. This could still be the intent, with Rei's body still appearing from the left for the sake of a strange sense of continuity (which is actually both discontinuous and continuous at the same time - discontinuous within the diegetic space and continuous in that Rei stays on the left of the screen.

Article: Screenshot framing question
 Sat Aug 22, 2009 6:05 am ARRRGGGHHH! Next time you post a thread like this Xard, MAKE SURE I'M AWAKE! After all the excellent analysis and interpretation that's been done I'm left with nothing to really do. :( I guess I can say this about the metro doors: It's interesting that tradition says to film imposing objects from a low angle, but it's almost as if those doors seem more menacing from a high angle. I wonder if Anno realized this? @Bonus Content: The object at the right in the first pic is most certainly a security camera; remember that's how Misato found out Shinji was there and, IIRC, she saw him from roughly the same angle. All of the interps on the Rei/Instrumentality shots were fascinating. I'd never thought much about this before but the whole thing has a very distinct quality where it is detached but perfectly continuous at the same time which gives it the quality of both fantasy and reality, of something really happening, but feeling as if it's more dreamlike.
